content.text: A launch checklist is only as reliable as the clarity of its ownership. A checklist item with no named owner is a checklist item that will not get done. A checklist item with three nominal owners and no clear accountability is in the same situation. The RACI matrix -- Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed -- is the most widely deployed framework for making task ownership explicit in multi-team launch processes. This sub-subtopic covers how to construct a RACI matrix for a GenAI service launch checklist, the anti-patterns that corrupt ownership clarity, the tooling that tracks owners at scale, and the escalation mechanisms that fire when owners fall behind.

GenAI service launches involve more disciplines than conventional software launches: engineering, SRE, security, legal, privacy, product, communications, and executive sponsor all have genuine roles. When that many stakeholders are involved, ownership gaps and ownership conflicts are almost inevitable without a formal model. The RACI matrix provides the model.
What It Is
RACI is an acronym for the four roles a person or team can hold with respect to a task or decision:
Responsible (R)

: the person or team who does the actual work to complete the task. There should be at least one R per row. Having multiple R assignments is acceptable but each R must have a clear scope of what they are doing.
Accountable (A)

: the single person who owns the outcome and signs off on completion. There must be exactly one A per row -- never zero, never more than one. The A delegates to the R but cannot delegate the accountability itself. If the task fails, A is answerable.
Consulted (C)
: stakeholders whose input is required before the task is completed. Communication is two-way. The R must solicit and incorporate input from C; C must respond within a defined timeframe.
Informed (I)
: stakeholders who need to know the outcome or status, but whose input is not required. Communication is one-way: results are pushed to I after decisions are made, not before.

The RACI matrix is a table where rows are tasks or deliverables from the launch checklist and columns are roles or named individuals. Each cell contains one or more of R, A, C, I -- or is blank when a role has no involvement in that task. A well-formed RACI has exactly one A per row and at least one R per row.
Why It Matters
Without explicit ownership assignments, three failure modes emerge in complex launches. First,
diffusion of responsibility
: everyone assumes someone else is handling a task because multiple people were mentioned in the kickoff meeting. Second,
missing coverage
: a task falls through the cracks because it sat at the boundary between two teams and neither believed it was their responsibility. Third,
approval bottlenecks
: a task is complete but stuck waiting for sign-off because it was never clear who had the authority to approve it.

For agentic AI launches, these failure modes carry disproportionate consequence. A missed security sign-off means a system with tool-use capabilities launches without a verified threat model. A missing legal review means an AI that processes customer data may launch without a required data processing agreement. A missing SRE sign-off means the team may not have agreed on an SLO or on the runbook for recovering from a model API outage. The RACI matrix is the mechanism that prevents these gaps by making every coverage obligation explicit before the launch date.
Benefits
Eliminates ambiguity
: every task has a named, accountable owner. 'We thought you were handling it' is not a valid post-mortem finding when RACI was defined and agreed upfront.
Appropriate authority
: separating Accountable from Responsible allows senior leaders to own outcomes without doing the work themselves, while keeping execution with the team members closest to the details.
Prevents over-consultation
: by explicitly marking some stakeholders as Informed rather than Consulted, RACI reduces the meeting overhead of including everyone in every decision.
Audit trail
: a documented RACI is evidence that ownership decisions were made deliberately before launch, not reconstructed after an incident.
Escalation clarity
: when a task is blocked or behind schedule, the A column tells you exactly who to escalate to -- no organizational navigation required.
Onboarding acceleration
: new team members can understand what they are accountable for and who the relevant stakeholders are for each of their tasks by reading the RACI, without needing a lengthy handoff meeting.
Trade-offs

RACI matrices are frequently derided as bureaucratic overhead by engineering teams that have experienced poorly-run RACI processes. The criticism is legitimate when the matrix is too granular (every subtask has its own row), too coarse (broad domains rather than specific deliverables), or defined once and never updated as the launch plan evolves.
RACI does not replace communication
: a team that defines RACI and then stops talking to each other has not solved coordination; it has documented it. RACI defines who talks to whom, not that they talk well.
Maintenance cost
: as the launch checklist evolves, the RACI must evolve with it. A RACI that reflects a plan from three weeks ago is more harmful than no RACI because it provides false confidence about coverage.
Cannot encode partial responsibilities
: RACI is binary -- you are C or you are not. For tasks where two teams share a continuous responsibility, RACI requires an additional coordination agreement beyond what the matrix captures.
Not suitable for very small teams
: a two-person startup does not need a RACI matrix. The overhead is justified when three or more teams are involved with different accountability structures.
How It Works
Building a RACI matrix for a launch checklist proceeds in four steps:
Enumerate the deliverables
: list every task from the launch checklist as a row. Tasks should be specific enough that completion is unambiguous. 'Security' is not a row; 'Security team sign-off on threat model' is.
Enumerate the roles

: list every team or role involved in the launch as a column. Use role labels rather than personal names where possible; personal names create a RACI that breaks the moment someone is on vacation or leaves the company.
Fill in the cells
: for each task, assign R, A, C, or I. Start with A (exactly one per row) and R (at least one per row). Then identify which other teams need to provide input (C) or need to be notified (I).
Validate and agree

: review the RACI with all stakeholders in a single meeting. Each team lead must explicitly confirm their assignments. Any cell that a team lead disagrees with must be resolved before the meeting ends.

The completed RACI is then stored where all stakeholders can access it (a shared document, a project management tool, or a version-controlled file), linked from the launch checklist, and reviewed at each launch readiness sync to verify it reflects the current plan.
Architecture

The diagram below shows a RACI matrix for key launch checklist tasks, illustrating R/A/C/I assignments across roles, plus common anti-patterns to avoid when constructing ownership matrices for GenAI service launches.

attrs.alt: Launch Checklist RACI - Ownership Architecture showing task ownership matrix and common anti-patterns

content.text: When to Use It
When to use RACI
Three or more teams are involved in a launch with different accountability structures.
Previous launches experienced coverage gaps or ownership disputes post-launch.
The launch involves cross-functional sign-offs (security, legal, compliance, finance) where a missed sign-off has regulatory consequences.
The launch checklist has more than 20 tasks spread across more than 3 teams.
The launch timeline is tight and there is no margin for 'who owns this?' conversations during the final week.
When NOT to use RACI
A single team owns the entire launch end-to-end. RACI overhead is not justified when there is no inter-team coordination problem to solve.
The launch is a minor update with a checklist of fewer than 10 tasks. A simple owner field on each checklist item is sufficient.
The team cannot commit to maintaining the RACI as the launch plan changes. An outdated RACI is worse than no RACI.
How to Implement

The practical implementation of RACI for a GenAI launch checklist involves three artifacts: the RACI document itself, tracked tasks in a project management tool with owner and accountable fields, and an escalation trigger mechanism for slipping tasks.

The RACI document should be the source of truth. Store it as a YAML or CSV file in version control alongside the PRR checklist, so changes are tracked and reviewable. An alternative is a dedicated sheet in the project's shared workspace, linked from the launch ticket.
Each task in the RACI should map directly to a ticket or issue in your project management tool (Jira, Linear, Asana, GitHub Issues). The ticket should have an
assignee
(the R) and a
custom field for Accountable
(the A). Setting a due date on each ticket and configuring an automated reminder 48 hours before the due date eliminates the need for manual tracking of every item.

The escalation mechanism is the most important operational piece. When a task's due date passes without completion, the system should automatically notify the A (not just the R), and optionally notify the A's manager after a further 24-hour grace period. This creates a forcing function that prevents slippage from accumulating silently until the week before launch.
Examples
RACI Matrix as YAML

# raci/launch-raci.yaml
# RACI matrix for customer-support-agent-v2 launch
# Columns: eng_lead, sre_ops, security, legal, product, comms, exec_sponsor

tasks:
  - id: prr-completion
    label: "PRR completion and sign-off"
    eng_lead:     A
    sre_ops:      R
    security:     C
    legal:        C
    product:      I
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: I

  - id: security-signoff
    label: "Security team threat model sign-off"
    eng_lead:     A
    sre_ops:      I
    security:     R
    legal:        I
    product:      I
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: I

  - id: load-testing
    label: "Load testing at 2x expected peak traffic"
    eng_lead:     A
    sre_ops:      R
    security:     I
    legal:        I
    product:      I
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: I

  - id: legal-compliance
    label: "Legal and compliance review (DPA, AI Act)"
    eng_lead:     A
    sre_ops:      I
    security:     C
    legal:        R
    product:      C
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: I

  - id: launch-go-no-go
    label: "Launch go/no-go decision"
    eng_lead:     I
    sre_ops:      C
    security:     C
    legal:        C
    product:      R
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: A

  - id: customer-comms
    label: "Customer launch communication draft approved"
    eng_lead:     I
    sre_ops:      I
    security:     I
    legal:        C
    product:      C
    comms:        R
    exec_sponsor: A

  - id: runbook-verified
    label: "Operational runbook reviewed and verified"
    eng_lead:     A
    sre_ops:      R
    security:     I
    legal:        I
    product:      I
    comms:        I
    exec_sponsor: I
Jira Automation: Auto-assign Owners from RACI YAML

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# sync_raci_to_jira.py
# Reads raci/launch-raci.yaml and ensures each task has the correct
# Jira ticket assignee (R) and Accountable custom field (A).
# Requires: jira-python>=3.0, PyYAML

import yaml
from jira import JIRA

JIRA_URL = "https://yourcompany.atlassian.net"
JIRA_TOKEN = "your-api-token"       # Use env var in production
JIRA_EMAIL = "automation@company.com"
PROJECT_KEY = "LAUNCH"

# Maps RACI role labels to Jira user account IDs
ROLE_TO_JIRA_USER = {
    "eng_lead":     "accountid:abc123",
    "sre_ops":      "accountid:def456",
    "security":     "accountid:ghi789",
    "legal":        "accountid:jkl012",
    "product":      "accountid:mno345",
    "comms":        "accountid:pqr678",
    "exec_sponsor": "accountid:stu901",
}

ACCOUNTABLE_FIELD = "customfield_10042"

def get_responsible_roles(task: dict) -> list:
    return [role for role, assignment in task.items()
            if role in ROLE_TO_JIRA_USER and assignment == "R"]

def get_accountable_role(task: dict) -> str:
    for role, assignment in task.items():
        if role in ROLE_TO_JIRA_USER and assignment == "A":
            return role
    return None

def sync_raci_to_jira(raci_path: str):
    jira = JIRA(server=JIRA_URL, basic_auth=(JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_TOKEN))

    with open(raci_path) as f:
        raci = yaml.safe_load(f)

    for task in raci["tasks"]:
        task_id = task["id"]

        issues = jira.search_issues(
            f'project={PROJECT_KEY} AND labels="{task_id}" AND statusCategory != Done',
            maxResults=1
        )
        if not issues:
            print(f"[WARN] No Jira issue found for task: {task_id}")
            continue

        issue = issues[0]
        responsible_roles = get_responsible_roles(task)
        accountable_role = get_accountable_role(task)

        if responsible_roles:
            primary_r = responsible_roles[0]
            jira.assign_issue(issue, ROLE_TO_JIRA_USER[primary_r])
            print(f"[OK] {task_id}: assignee -> {primary_r}")

        if accountable_role:
            jira.issue(issue.id).update(
                fields={ACCOUNTABLE_FIELD: {"accountId": ROLE_TO_JIRA_USER[accountable_role].split(":")[1]}}
            )
            print(f"[OK] {task_id}: accountable -> {accountable_role}")

if __name__ == "__main__":
    sync_raci_to_jira("raci/launch-raci.yaml")
Escalation Trigger: Notify Accountable When Deadline Passes

#!/usr/bin/env python3
# escalate_overdue_tasks.py
# Runs daily as a cron job. Checks all open launch checklist tickets
# for overdue items. Notifies the Accountable person via Slack when a
# task is past its due date. Escalates to manager after 24h grace period.

import os
import datetime
from jira import JIRA
import requests

JIRA_URL = "https://yourcompany.atlassian.net"
JIRA_TOKEN = os.environ["JIRA_API_TOKEN"]
JIRA_EMAIL = os.environ["JIRA_EMAIL"]
SLACK_WEBHOOK = os.environ["SLACK_ESCALATION_WEBHOOK"]
PROJECT_KEY = "LAUNCH"
ACCOUNTABLE_FIELD = "customfield_10042"
MANAGER_FIELD = "customfield_10099"

jira = JIRA(server=JIRA_URL, basic_auth=(JIRA_EMAIL, JIRA_TOKEN))
today = datetime.date.today()

def slack_notify(message: str):
    requests.post(SLACK_WEBHOOK, json={"text": message})

def check_overdue_tasks():
    overdue_issues = jira.search_issues(
        f'project={PROJECT_KEY} '
        f'AND statusCategory != Done '
        f'AND due < "{today.isoformat()}" '
        f'AND labels = "launch-checklist"',
        maxResults=100
    )

    for issue in overdue_issues:
        due_date = datetime.date.fromisoformat(issue.fields.duedate)
        days_overdue = (today - due_date).days

        accountable_user = getattr(issue.fields, ACCOUNTABLE_FIELD, None)
        if not accountable_user:
            slack_notify(
                f":warning: *{issue.key}*: '{issue.fields.summary}' is {days_overdue}d overdue "
                f"and has NO Accountable person assigned. Fix RACI immediately."
            )
            continue

        a_name = accountable_user.get("displayName", "Unknown")
        a_id = accountable_user.get("accountId", "")

        if days_overdue == 1:
            slack_notify(
                f":rotating_light: *Launch task overdue* -- "
                f"<@{a_id}> ({a_name}) you are Accountable for:\n"
                f"*{issue.key}*: {issue.fields.summary}\n"
                f"Due: {issue.fields.duedate} ({days_overdue}d ago). "
                f"Please update status or request a date extension."
            )
        elif days_overdue >= 2:
            manager_user = getattr(issue.fields, MANAGER_FIELD, None)
            manager_mention = (
                f"<@{manager_user.get('accountId')}>"
                if manager_user else "Engineering Director"
            )
            slack_notify(
                f":sos: *Escalation: launch checklist task {days_overdue}d overdue*\n"
                f"Accountable: <@{a_id}> ({a_name}) | Manager: {manager_mention}\n"
                f"*{issue.key}*: {issue.fields.summary}\n"
                f"Due: {issue.fields.duedate}. Immediate action required."
            )

if __name__ == "__main__":
    check_overdue_tasks()
Real-World Example

A large healthcare technology company launching an AI-assisted clinical documentation service in 2023 used a RACI matrix with eight columns (engineering, SRE, security, legal, clinical informatics, compliance, product, executive sponsor) and 34 checklist rows. During the launch readiness review at T-3 weeks, the RACI review identified that the
clinical informatics team

was Consulted on NLP output quality review but had no Responsible assignment for verifying that the AI's medical terminology was clinically accurate. The task had been assumed to belong to engineering, who lacked clinical expertise.

Catching this in the RACI review three weeks before launch allowed the clinical informatics lead to assign a clinical analyst (R) and schedule two weeks of structured review. Without the explicit RACI review, this gap would almost certainly have appeared as a post-launch quality incident affecting clinicians who relied on the documentation suggestions. The RACI review meeting, which took 90 minutes, prevented what would have been a multi-week remediation effort and potential regulatory notification.

A second example: Spotify's engineering culture documented in their Squad Model papers (2012) made RACI-like ownership explicit without using the RACI name. Each squad owned outcomes (Accountable) for its domain. Chapter leads were Consulted on technical standards. Tribes were Informed on cross-squad decisions. When launching cross-squad features, the model required explicit RACI extensions because the standard squad ownership model did not cover the boundary cases. Teams that neglected this extension step reported the highest frequency of launch-day incidents attributable to ownership gaps.
Common Mistakes
Multiple Accountables per row (shared A)
: when two people are both marked A, neither is truly accountable. This is the most common RACI error and the most damaging. There must be exactly one A per row.
No Responsible assigned (no R)
: someone is marked A but there is no R. The accountable person will have to do the work themselves or the task will not get done. Every A row needs at least one R.
Overloaded R
: one engineer is Responsible for 80% of the tasks. This creates a single point of failure and a bottleneck. Distribute R assignments proportionally.
Exec marked Informed on go/no-go
: the executive sponsor should be Accountable on the launch go/no-go decision, not merely Informed. An executive who is only notified of the launch decision has no accountability for it.
RACI defined once and never updated
: as team members change, tasks are added, or scope changes, the RACI becomes outdated. Review and update the RACI at each launch readiness sync.
Using personal names instead of roles
: naming a specific person as A means the RACI breaks the moment that person takes leave. Use role labels (Eng Lead, SRE Lead) and document the current holder separately.
No response SLA for Consulted stakeholders
: marking a team as Consulted without specifying a response timeline guarantees the consultation will be ignored or late. C must have a deadline.
Best Practices
Enforce exactly one A per row
: validate this programmatically in CI when RACI is stored as YAML. Reject matrices that violate the rule.
Define a response SLA for Consulted stakeholders
: C must respond within 48 hours during the launch window. Document this expectation when the RACI is agreed.
Store RACI in version control
: YAML or CSV alongside the PRR checklist ensures every change is tracked, reviewable, and auditable.
Link every RACI row to a project management ticket
: the RACI is the planning artifact; the ticket is the execution artifact. Without the link, RACI status and ticket status diverge silently.
Review the RACI at every launch readiness sync
: confirm assignments are still accurate as the launch plan and team composition evolve.
Automate escalation for overdue A items
: the A should be notified automatically when their task is overdue, not wait for a status meeting.
Conduct a RACI retrospective after launch
: identify tasks where assignments were wrong, unclear, or missing, and update the template for the next launch.
Performance and Scalability
At scale -- multiple simultaneous launches, large organizations, many active checklists -- the RACI matrix requires automation to remain useful.
Templated RACI per service tier

: maintain tier-specific templates (e.g., Tier-1 GenAI service RACI, Tier-2 internal tool RACI) that can be instantiated quickly and adapted per-launch rather than building a new RACI from scratch each time.
RACI as code

: storing RACI as YAML allows CI/CD pipelines to validate structure, generate dashboards, and sync assignments to Jira automatically. Manual RACI management does not scale beyond about five simultaneous launches.
Delegation chains

: for large organizations, document who fills each role when the primary holder is unavailable. A RACI that depends on a single named person who is on vacation during the launch is a reliability risk.
Cross-launch visibility
: when multiple launches are in flight simultaneously, a dashboard showing RACI coverage and overdue items across all active launches helps program managers identify resource conflicts early.
Tooling and Ecosystem
GitHub / GitLab

: store RACI YAML files in the repo alongside the PRR checklist. Use GitHub Actions to validate RACI structure on PR merge (check for exactly one A per row, no missing R rows) and post a coverage report.
Jira
: add Accountable as a custom field on launch checklist issues. Use Jira automation rules to notify the A when an issue becomes overdue. Dashboards can show open items by A, grouped by severity.
Linear
: use team assignments for R and a custom property for A. Filters by team and status provide a per-team view of outstanding launch obligations.
Confluence / Notion
: RACI matrices in table format work well in collaborative docs for initial construction and stakeholder review. Export to YAML for version control once agreed.
Slack / MS Teams automation
: webhook-based escalation scripts (as shown in the examples above) send targeted notifications to the A when tasks slip, with escalation to managers after a grace period.
Spreadsheets (Google Sheets / Excel)
: widely used for initial RACI construction due to formatting flexibility. Acceptable for small teams; problematic for version control and automation at scale.
Comparison
Four frameworks for assigning ownership in multi-team deliverables:
Framework
Roles
Strengths
Weaknesses
Best for
RACI
Responsible, Accountable, Consulted, Informed
Widely understood; clear accountability separation; exactly-one-A rule prevents shared accountability; works at any scale
Does not differentiate driver vs. approver; Consulted response SLA must be defined separately
Cross-functional launches with 3+ teams, regulatory sign-offs, GenAI service PRRs
DACI
Driver, Approver, Contributor, Informed
Explicit Driver role clarifies who is running the process vs. who owns the outcome; Approver is clearer than A about decision authority
Less familiar than RACI; does not distinguish work-doers from input-providers as explicitly as RACI does
Decision-heavy processes where the meeting facilitator and the outcome approver need to be explicitly separated
RAPID
Recommend, Agree, Perform, Input, Decide
Very fine-grained role definitions; distinguishes who recommends from who decides; strong for structured decision-making
Complex and unfamiliar; five roles create ambiguity in practice; requires significant team training; suited to strategy, not operational checklists
Strategic cross-organizational decisions at the executive level where role clarity is critical and the audience is trained in the framework
Simple Owner-Assignee
Owner (accountable), Assignee (does work)
Minimal overhead; easy to implement in any project management tool; immediately understandable
No mechanism for Consulted stakeholders; no Informed distinction; ownership gaps at team boundaries are not surfaced
Small single-team launches with fewer than 10 tasks and no cross-functional sign-offs
Security and Reliability

The RACI matrix has direct security implications in agentic AI launches. Security sign-off tasks without a named Responsible are the tasks most likely to be missed. Missed security sign-offs on systems with autonomous tool-use capabilities or access to sensitive data are the category of launch gap most likely to produce a significant incident.

From a reliability perspective, the most important RACI design decision is ensuring that the SRE or operations team has a Responsible assignment on runbook verification, load testing, and monitoring dashboard validation -- not just an Informed role. SRE teams that are only Informed on launch readiness tasks cannot exercise their operational judgment in time to affect the outcome.
Never mark security as Consulted-only on tasks where a gap could result in data loss or unauthorized access
. Security should be Responsible for verification tasks, not merely advisory.
Ensure the exec sponsor is Accountable on the go/no-go decision
. An exec who is Informed does not own the launch outcome in any meaningful sense.
Validate that every A can actually exercise their accountability during the launch window
: an A who is traveling or on leave needs a named backup before the RACI is finalized.
Review RACI coverage with the security team before the launch readiness meeting
: confirm every security-relevant checklist item has a named security team R, not just an engineering lead R with security as C.
Key Takeaways

RACI assigns four roles per task: Responsible (does the work), Accountable (owns the outcome, exactly one per row), Consulted (input required, two-way communication), and Informed (notified of outcomes, one-way).
The exactly-one-A rule is the most important structural constraint: shared accountability is no accountability. Validate this programmatically when RACI is stored as code.
GenAI service launches involve security, legal, SRE, product, and exec stakeholders who all have genuine roles; RACI makes every coverage obligation explicit before the launch date.
Store the RACI as YAML in version control and sync it automatically to your project management tool so assignments are reflected in executable tickets with due dates and assignees.
Automate escalation: when a task deadline passes, notify the A automatically -- not just the R. The A is accountable for the outcome, which means they must know when things are slipping.
Common anti-patterns to avoid: multiple A per row, no R per row, overloaded R on one engineer, exec marked Informed on go/no-go, RACI defined once and never updated.
Consulted stakeholders need a defined response SLA; a C with no deadline is effectively an I and provides no actual input protection.
Conduct a RACI retrospective after each launch to improve ownership clarity for the next one and update the template for the service tier.
